---
title: "Inference and Intervention"
subtitle: "Causal Models for Business Analysis, Applied to Maternal & Newborn Health"
---

## Course Overview

This course adapts the frameworks from *Inference and Intervention: Causal Models for Business Analysis* (Ryall & Bramson, 2014) for professionals working in maternal and newborn health (MNH) programs. Across 10 chapters, you will build a complete toolkit for causal reasoning — from constructing qualitative models to making data-driven resource allocation decisions.

::: {.callout-box}
**The central message:** Causal models are not just academic abstractions — they are **practical tools** for answering the questions that matter most in global health: *Where should we invest? What should we do? How do we know it will work?*
:::

## The MNH Analyst's Workflow

By the end of this course, you will be able to follow this evidence-based workflow:

1. **Build a DAG** from expert interviews and program theory (Chapters 2--3)
2. **Quantify** the model with DHIS2 and program data (Chapters 4--5, 10)
3. **Avoid causal reasoning traps** like Simpson's Paradox (Chapter 6)
4. **Make allocation decisions** using decision analysis and do-calculus (Chapters 7--8)
5. **Anticipate strategic responses** from implementing partners (Chapter 9)
6. **Validate and refine** with observational data and structure learning (Chapter 10)

## Chapters

### Part I: Foundations

| Chapter | Title | Focus |
|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | [Introduction to Causal Analysis](chapters/ch01.qmd) | Situational assessment vs. intervention; correlation ≠ causation |
| 2 | [Qualitative Causal Models](chapters/ch02.qmd) | Nodes, directed links, triplet structures, d-separation |
| 3 | [The MNH Diagnostic Case Study](chapters/ch03.qmd) | Building a complete DAG from stakeholder interviews |

### Part II: Quantitative Analysis

| Chapter | Title | Focus |
|---------|-------|-------|
| 4 | [Quantitative Causal Models](chapters/ch04.qmd) | Conditional probability tables, Bayes' theorem, parameterization |
| 5 | [Situational Analysis](chapters/ch05.qmd) | Belief updating, inference, evidence propagation |
| 6 | [Simpson's Paradox & Cost-Effectiveness](chapters/ch06.qmd) | Aggregation paradoxes, causal reasoning in cost-effectiveness |

### Part III: Decision & Strategy

| Chapter | Title | Focus |
|---------|-------|-------|
| 7 | [Decision Analysis & Value of Information](chapters/ch07.qmd) | Do-calculus, expected utility, value of information |
| 8 | [Multi-Country Resource Allocation](chapters/ch08.qmd) | Portfolio optimization under uncertainty across countries |
| 9 | [Game Theory & Strategic Interactions](chapters/ch09.qmd) | Nash equilibrium, incentive design, multi-agent dynamics |

### Part IV: Data-Driven Methods

| Chapter | Title | Focus |
|---------|-------|-------|
| 10 | [Structure Learning from Observational Data](chapters/ch10.qmd) | Markov equivalence, constraint/score-based learning, instrumental variables |

## The Complete Toolkit

| Chapters | Tool |
|----------|------|
| 1--3 | Qualitative DAGs |
| 4--5 | Bayesian networks & inference |
| 6 | Causal reasoning pitfalls |
| 7 | Decision analysis & do-calculus |
| 8 | Resource allocation under uncertainty |
| 9 | Multi-agent game theory |
| 10 | Data-driven discovery |

## Prerequisites

- Basic familiarity with probability and statistics
- Introductory R programming (see the [R Setup](r-setup.qmd) page for installation and packages)
- No prior knowledge of causal inference is assumed

## Textbook

Ryall, M. D., & Bramson, A. (2014). *Inference and Intervention: Causal Models for Business Analysis*. Routledge.

::: {.callout-box .green}
**For MNH program managers:** This toolkit enables rigorous allocation of pooled funding across multiple countries, with clear reasoning about *why* each investment is expected to save lives — and honest acknowledgment of what we don't know.
:::
